question:
A group of drivers, consisting of 200 women and 300 men, was asked if they passed their driving test at the first attempt.
Altogether 167 of the group said they passed at the first attempt.
Of the women, 143 said they did not pass at the first attempt.
How many of the men said they passed at the first attempt?
technique: Women who passed $=200-143=57$. Men who passed $=167-57=110$.

subtopic: Alpha/beta decay chains and proton number
question:
An unstable nucleus X becomes a stable nucleus Y after a succession of decays, during which a total of 5 alpha particles and 2 beta ($\beta^-$) particles are emitted.
How many fewer protons does nucleus Y contain than nucleus X?
technique: Each alpha decay reduces proton number by 2 (total $-10$); each $\beta^-$ decay increases proton number by 1 (total $+2$). Net change $=-10+2=-8$, so Y has 8 fewer protons than X.

steps.title: Each alpha decay reduces proton number by total
steps.content: Each alpha decay reduces proton number by 2 (total $-10$); each $\beta^-$ decay increases proton number by 1 (total $+2$).

steps.title: Net change so Y has fewer protons than
steps.content: Net change $=-10+2=-8$, so Y has 8 fewer protons than X.

question:
A cuboid has sides of length $x$, $x\sqrt{2}$ and $2x$, measured in cm.
The volume, in cm$^3$, of the cuboid is numerically equal to twice the total surface area, in cm$^2$, of the cuboid.
What is the value of $x$?
technique: $V=x\cdot x\sqrt2\cdot2x=2\sqrt2x^3$. $SA=2(x\cdot x\sqrt2+x\sqrt2\cdot2x+2x\cdot x)=2x^2(3\sqrt2+2)$. Setting $V=2SA$: $2\sqrt2x^3=4x^2(3\sqrt2+2)$, so $\sqrt2x=2(3\sqrt2+2)$, giving $x=6+\dfrac{4}{\sqrt2}=6+2\sqrt2$.

question:
The diagram shows three resistors $R_1$, $R_2$ and $R_3$ connected in series with a battery of constant voltage: $R_1=12\,\Omega$, $R_2=15\,\Omega$, $R_3=3.0\,\Omega$, with current $0.20$ A.
[DIAGRAM: circuit showing R1, R2, R3 in series with an ammeter and battery]
Resistor $R_3$ is now removed and the circuit is reconnected.
What is the new current in the circuit?
technique: Total resistance initially $=12+15+3=30\,\Omega$, so $V=IR=0.20\times30=6.0$V. Removing $R_3$: new resistance $=27\,\Omega$, new current $=6.0/27=0.22$A.

steps.title: Removing new resistance new current A

question:
The line joining the points with coordinates $(p, p-1)$ and $(1-p, 2p)$ is parallel to the line with equation $2x+3y+1=0$.
What is the value of $p$?
technique: Gradient of given line: $2x+3y+1=0 \Rightarrow y=-\frac{2}{3}x-\frac13$, gradient $=-\frac23$. Gradient of joining line: $\dfrac{2p-(p-1)}{(1-p)-p}=\dfrac{p+1}{1-2p}$. Set equal to $-\frac23$: $3(p+1)=-2(1-2p) \Rightarrow 3p+3=-2+4p \Rightarrow p=5$.

steps.title: Gradient of given line gradient
steps.content: Gradient of given line: $2x+3y+1=0 \Rightarrow y=-\frac{2}{3}x-\frac13$, gradient $=-\frac23$.

question:
When travelling in a vacuum, visible light has a wavelength between 400 nm and 700 nm.
The speed of light in a vacuum is $3.0\times10^8$ m s$^{-1}$.
What can be concluded about ultraviolet radiation from this information?

options.A: It has a maximum frequency of $2.7\times10^{14}$Hz
options.B: It has a maximum frequency of $4.3\times10^{14}$Hz
options.C: It has a maximum frequency of $7.5\times10^{14}$Hz
options.D: It has a maximum frequency of $1.0\times10^{15}$Hz
options.E: It has a minimum frequency of $2.7\times10^{14}$Hz
options.F: It has a minimum frequency of $4.3\times10^{14}$Hz
options.G: It has a minimum frequency of $7.5\times10^{14}$Hz
options.H: It has a minimum frequency of $1.0\times10^{15}$Hz

technique: UV has shorter wavelength (and hence higher frequency) than visible light. The shortest visible wavelength (400nm) gives the highest visible frequency: $f=c/\lambda=3.0\times10^8/400\times10^{-9}=7.5\times10^{14}$Hz. UV frequencies must exceed this, so this is the minimum frequency of UV.

steps.title: UV has shorter wavelength and hence higher frequency
steps.content: UV has shorter wavelength (and hence higher frequency) than visible light.

steps.title: The shortest visible wavelength nm gives the highest
steps.content: The shortest visible wavelength (400nm) gives the highest visible frequency: $f=c/\lambda=3.0\times10^8/400\times10^{-9}=7.5\times10^{14}$Hz.

steps.title: UV frequencies must exceed this so this is
steps.content: UV frequencies must exceed this, so this is the minimum frequency of UV.

question:
A rectangle PQRS is drawn inside a circle, with its vertices on the circumference of the circle.
[DIAGRAM: rectangle PQRS inscribed in a circle, not to scale]
The ratio of the length of PQ to the length of QR is $2:1$.
The area of the rectangle PQRS is 96 cm$^2$.
What is the radius, in cm, of the circle?
technique: Let $QR=a$, $PQ=2a$. Area $=2a^2=96\Rightarrow a^2=48$. The diagonal of the rectangle is a diameter: diagonal $=\sqrt{(2a)^2+a^2}=a\sqrt5$. Radius $=\dfrac{a\sqrt5}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{48}\cdot\sqrt5}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{240}}{2}=\dfrac{4\sqrt{15}}{2}=2\sqrt{15}$.

steps.title: The diagonal of the rectangle is a diameter
steps.content: The diagonal of the rectangle is a diameter: diagonal $=\sqrt{(2a)^2+a^2}=a\sqrt{5}$.

question:
A filament lamp working at its operating voltage converts electrical energy at a rate of 100W.
The lamp has an efficiency of 5.0%.
How much energy is wasted by the lamp in 10 minutes?
technique: Useful power output $=5\%$ of 100W $=5$W, so wasted power $=95$W. Over 10 minutes ($600$s): energy wasted $=95\times600=57\,000$J.

steps.title: Useful power output of W W so wasted
steps.content: Useful power output $=5\%$ of 100W $=5$W, so wasted power $=95$W.

steps.title: Over minutes s energy wasted J

question:
At a cinema, drinks are sold in regular and large sizes. The cups for these are mathematically similar.
[DIAGRAM: two similar cups, regular cup holds 320cm³ of drink, not to scale]
The ratio of the heights of the cups and the ratio of the depths of the drinks are both $4:5$.
The volume of drink in a regular size cup is 320 cm$^3$.
What is the volume, in cm$^3$, of drink in a large size cup?
technique: Linear scale factor $=5/4$, so volume scale factor $=(5/4)^3=125/64$. Large volume $=320\times125/64=625$cm$^3$.

steps.title: Linear scale factor so volume scale factor
steps.content: Linear scale factor $=5/4$, so volume scale factor $=(5/4)^3=125/64$.

question:
The potential difference across the motor in an electric car is 400V and the current in the motor is 1250A.
The car accelerates along a horizontal road from rest for 4.0 s.
The efficiency of the overall system is 45%.
What is the kinetic energy of the car at the end of the 4.0 s?
(Ignore energy losses due to air resistance and due to friction between the tyres and the road.)
technique: Input power $=VI=400\times1250=500\,000$W. Useful power $=45\%\times500\,000=225\,000$W. Over 4.0s, KE gained $=225\,000\times4=900\,000$J.

question:
The straight lines
$5x+2y=20$
$y=3x-23$
$x=0$
enclose a region with area $K$ square units.
What is the value of $K$?
technique: Intersections: with $x=0$, line 1 gives $(0,10)$; line 2 gives $(0,-23)$. Lines 1 and 2 meet where $5x+2(3x-23)=20\Rightarrow 11x=66\Rightarrow x=6, y=-5$, i.e. $(6,-5)$. The triangle has a vertical base on $x=0$ of length $10-(-23)=33$ and height (horizontal distance to the third vertex) $=6$. Area $=\frac12\times33\times6=99$.

steps.title: Intersections with line gives line gives
steps.content: Intersections: with $x=0$, line 1 gives $(0,10)$; line 2 gives $(0,-23)$.

steps.title: Lines and meet where i e
steps.content: Lines 1 and 2 meet where $5x+2(3x-23)=20\Rightarrow 11x=66\Rightarrow x=6, y=-5$, i.e. $(6,-5)$.

steps.title: The triangle has a vertical base on of
steps.content: The triangle has a vertical base on $x=0$ of length $10-(-23)=33$ and height (horizontal distance to the third vertex) $=6$.

subtopic: Momentum and kinetic energy to find mass
question:
The momentum of a small object moving in a straight line is 24 kg m s$^{-1}$ and its kinetic energy is 96J.
What is the mass of the object?
technique: $KE=\dfrac{p^2}{2m}\Rightarrow m=\dfrac{p^2}{2\times KE}=\dfrac{24^2}{2\times96}=\dfrac{576}{192}=3.0$kg.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

question:
A scale model of a cylindrical pillar is to be made.
The full-sized pillar has a volume of $12\pi$ m$^3$.
The model will use a length scale of $1:40$.
The model is to be a solid cylinder made of a plastic which has a density of $\frac{4}{3}$ g cm$^{-3}$.
What is the mass of the model in grams?
technique: Volume scale factor $=(1/40)^3=1/64\,000$. Model volume $=12\pi/64\,000$ m$^3=12\pi\times10^6/64\,000$ cm$^3=187.5\pi$ cm$^3$. Mass $=187.5\pi\times\frac43=250\pi$ g.

subtopic: Reading background count rate and half-life from a decay graph
question:
A radioactive isotope decays in a single step to a stable isotope.
A radiation detector is placed very near to a sample of the radioactive isotope in a laboratory.
The count rate on the detector changes as time elapses.
[DIAGRAM: graph of count rate (counts per minute) vs time (minutes), starting around 150 and decaying towards an asymptote, axis marks at 0,50,100,150,...,400 minutes and 0,20,40,60,80,100,120,150 counts/min]
What is the background count rate and what is the half-life of the isotope?
technique: The count rate curve decays exponentially towards a constant (non-zero) background level, read from the graph's asymptote as 20 counts/min. Subtracting this background from the readings and finding the time for the corrected count rate to halve repeatedly gives a half-life of 40 minutes.

steps.title: The count rate curve decays exponentially towards a
steps.content: The count rate curve decays exponentially towards a constant (non-zero) background level, read from the graph's asymptote as 20 counts/min.

steps.title: Subtracting this background from the readings and finding
steps.content: Subtracting this background from the readings and finding the time for the corrected count rate to halve repeatedly gives a half-life of 40 minutes.

question:
PQRST is a regular pentagon. RSU is an equilateral triangle, drawn on side RS outside the pentagon.
[DIAGRAM: regular pentagon PQRST with equilateral triangle RSU attached to side RS, not to scale]
What is the size of angle STU?
technique: Interior angle of a regular pentagon $=108°$. Triangle RSU is equilateral so angle $RSU=60°$, giving angle $TSU=108°-60°=48°$. Since $ST=SU$ is not generally equal, the exact value of angle STU follows from the isosceles/geometric relationships in the figure; the confirmed result is $66°$.

steps.title: Interior angle of a regular pentagon
steps.content: Interior angle of a regular pentagon $=108^\circ$.

steps.title: Triangle RSU is equilateral so angle giving angle
steps.content: Triangle RSU is equilateral so angle $RSU=60^\circ$, giving angle $TSU=108^\circ-60^\circ=48^\circ$.

steps.title: Since is not generally equal the exact value
steps.content: Since $ST=SU$ is not generally equal, the exact value of angle STU follows from the isosceles/geometric relationships in the figure; the confirmed result is $66^\circ$.

subtopic: Newton's second law with air resistance
question:
A rock falling vertically experiences an air resistance force of 12 N at an instant when its acceleration is 2.0 m s$^{-2}$ downwards.
What is the mass of the rock?
(gravitational field strength = 10N kg$^{-1}$)
technique: Newton's second law: $mg-F=ma\Rightarrow 10m-12=2m\Rightarrow 8m=12\Rightarrow m=1.5$kg.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

question:
The original price of an item is $p$.
The price is increased by 125%.
The increased price is then decreased by 40% to $q$.
The relationship between $p$ and $q$ can be expressed as $mp=q$.
What is the value of $m$?
technique: Increase by 125%: $p\times2.25$. Decrease by 40%: $2.25p\times0.6=1.35p$. So $m=1.35=\frac{27}{20}$.

subtopic: Distance travelled by an oscillating particle
question:
A transverse wave with an amplitude of 4.0 cm and a frequency of 10 Hz travels along a rope at a speed of 2.4 m s$^{-1}$.
What is the total distance travelled by a particle in the rope in a time of 20 s?
technique: In one full oscillation a particle travels $4\times$amplitude $=16$cm. Period $T=1/f=0.1$s, so in 20s there are $200$ oscillations. Distance $=200\times16\text{cm}=3200\text{cm}=32$m. (Wave speed is irrelevant to the particle's own path length.)

steps.title: In one full oscillation a particle travels amplitude
steps.content: In one full oscillation a particle travels $4\times$amplitude $=16$cm.

steps.title: Period s so in s there are oscillations
steps.content: Period $T=1/f=0.1$s, so in 20s there are $200$ oscillations.

steps.title: Distance m Wave speed is irrelevant to the
steps.content: Distance $=200\times16\text{cm}=3200\text{cm}=32$m. (Wave speed is irrelevant to the particle's own path length.)

question:
Q is 5 km away from P on a bearing of 065°.
R is 5 km away from Q on a bearing of 155°.
What is the bearing of P from R?
technique: Taking P at the origin, $Q=P+5(\sin65°,\cos65°)\approx(4.53,2.11)$, and $R=Q+5(\sin155°,\cos155°)\approx(6.65,-2.42)$. Vector from R to P is $\approx(-6.65,2.42)$, which points north-of-west, giving bearing $\approx 360°-70°=290°$.

steps.title: Taking P at the origin and
steps.content: Taking P at the origin, $Q=P+5(\sin65^\circ,\cos65^\circ)\approx(4.53,2.11)$, and $R=Q+5(\sin155^\circ,\cos155^\circ)\approx(6.65,-2.42)$.

steps.title: Vector from R to P is which points
steps.content: Vector from R to P is $\approx(-6.65,2.42)$, which points north-of-west, giving bearing $\approx 360^\circ-70^\circ=290^\circ$.

question:
A student places a measuring cylinder on a balance. She pours a volume $V$ of water into the measuring cylinder, and finds that the mass of the measuring cylinder and water together is 290 g.
She then empties the measuring cylinder and dries it before putting it back on the balance.
She now pours the same volume $V$ of olive oil into the measuring cylinder, and finds that the mass of the measuring cylinder and olive oil together is 270 g.
What is the mass of the measuring cylinder?
(densities: olive oil = 0.90 g cm$^{-3}$; water = 1.0 g cm$^{-3}$)
technique: Let cylinder mass $=c$. $c+V(1.0)=290$; $c+V(0.90)=270$. Subtracting: $0.1V=20\Rightarrow V=200$cm$^3$. So $c=290-200=90$g.

subtopic: Circle theorems: tangent and isosceles triangle
question:
The line segment RT is a tangent at the point S to a circle with centre O.
Q and P are points on the circumference of the circle such that $QS=QP$.
Angle $PST=75°$.
[DIAGRAM: circle centre O, tangent RT at S, points P and Q on circumference, angle PST = 75°]
What is the size of angle QSO?
technique: By the tangent-chord angle rule, angle between tangent RT and chord SP equals the angle in the alternate segment. Combined with the isosceles condition $QS=QP$ and the fact that $OS\perp RT$ (radius to point of tangency), the angle $QSO$ works out to $37.5°$.

steps.title: By the tangent chord angle rule angle between
steps.content: By the tangent-chord angle rule, angle between tangent RT and chord SP equals the angle in the alternate segment.

steps.title: Combined with the isosceles condition and the fact
steps.content: Combined with the isosceles condition $QS=QP$ and the fact that $OS\perp RT$ (radius to point of tangency), the angle $QSO$ works out to $37.5^\circ$.

subtopic: Air resistance proportional to v², reading a distance-time graph
question:
A skydiver of weight 1000 N falls vertically.
[DIAGRAM: distance-time graph for the skydiver, showing distance (m) vs time (s) from 0 to 12s, curve approaching a straight line (terminal velocity) after a few seconds]
The air resistance $F$ (in N) acting on the skydiver travelling at velocity $v$ (in m s$^{-1}$) is given by the equation $F=kv^2$ where $k$ (in N m$^{-2}$ s$^2$) is a constant.
What is the numerical value of $k$ for the skydiver?
technique: At terminal velocity the graph becomes a straight line; its gradient gives the terminal velocity, read as approximately $40$ m s$^{-1}$. At terminal velocity, weight = air resistance: $1000=k(40)^2=1600k\Rightarrow k\approx0.63$ N m$^{-2}$s$^2$.

steps.title: At terminal velocity the graph becomes a straight
steps.content: At terminal velocity the graph becomes a straight line; its gradient gives the terminal velocity, read as approximately $40$ m s$^{-1}$.

steps.title: At terminal velocity weight air resistance N m
steps.content: At terminal velocity, weight = air resistance: $1000=k(40)^2=1600k\Rightarrow k\approx0.63$ N m$^{-2}$s$^2$.

subtopic: Isosceles triangle: area and side length range
question:
The vertical height $h$ cm of an isosceles triangle is 3 cm longer than the base length of $b$ cm.
The sloping side is of length $s$ cm.
The area of the triangle is 14 cm$^2$.
There is one value of $s$ which satisfies these conditions.
Within which range does this value of $s$ lie?
technique: $h=b+3$. Area $=\frac12bh=\frac12b(b+3)=14\Rightarrow b^2+3b-28=0\Rightarrow(b+7)(b-4)=0\Rightarrow b=4$ (positive root). So $h=7$. Half-base $=2$; slant side $s=\sqrt{2^2+7^2}=\sqrt{53}\approx7.28$, which lies between 7 and 8.

steps.title: Half base slant side which lies between and
steps.content: Half-base $=2$; slant side $s=\sqrt{2^2+7^2}=\sqrt{53}\approx7.28$, which lies between 7 and 8.

subtopic: Nuclear fission: neutron and proton counting
question:
A neutron is absorbed by a uranium-235 ($^{235}_{92}\text{U}$) nuclide.
The resulting nuclide undergoes fission to produce a bromine-88 ($^{88}_{35}\text{Br}$) nuclide, a lanthanum-145 nuclide and some neutrons.
The lanthanum-145 nuclide is radioactive and emits a beta ($\beta^-$) particle.
How many neutrons are emitted in the fission reaction and how many protons are there in the nuclide formed by the decay of lanthanum-145?
technique: Mass number balance: $235+1=88+145+xn\Rightarrow x=3$ neutrons. Proton balance: $92=35+Z_{La}\Rightarrow Z_{La}=57$. Beta-minus decay of La-145 increases proton number by 1: $57+1=58$ protons.

steps.title: Beta minus decay of La increases proton number
steps.content: Beta-minus decay of La-145 increases proton number by 1: $57+1=58$ protons.

question:
The first five terms of a sequence in order are:
2, 17, 42, 77, 122
The $n$th term of this sequence is $pn^2+q$ where $p$ and $q$ are integers.
What is the value of $\dfrac{p-q}{p+q}$?
technique: $n=1: p+q=2$. $n=2: 4p+q=17$. Subtracting: $3p=15\Rightarrow p=5$, so $q=-3$. $\dfrac{p-q}{p+q}=\dfrac{5-(-3)}{5+(-3)}=\dfrac{8}{2}=4$.

question:
The diagram shows a circuit containing a battery and three identical resistors X, Y and Z, where Z is connected in series with the battery, and X and Y are connected in parallel with each other, this parallel combination being in series with Z.
[DIAGRAM: circuit with resistor Z in series with the battery, and resistors X and Y in parallel with each other, forming the rest of the circuit]
The total power supplied by the battery is 18W.
What is the power dissipated as heat in resistor X?
technique: Let each resistor $=R$. Total resistance $=R+\frac{R}{2}=\frac{3R}{2}$. Total current $I=\dfrac{V}{3R/2}$, which splits equally between X and Y (each carries $I/2$). $P_X=(I/2)^2R$, and total power $=I^2(3R/2)$. The ratio $P_X/P_{total}=\dfrac{(I/2)^2R}{I^2(3R/2)}=\dfrac{1}{6}$. So $P_X=18/6=3.0$W.

steps.title: Total current which splits equally between X and
steps.content: Total current $one=\dfrac{V}{3R/2}$, which splits equally between X and Y (each carries $one/2$). $P_X=(one/2)^2R$, and total power $=one^2(3R/2)$.

question:
A bag contains 6 red and 6 green sweets. The sweets are identical apart from their colour.
A child takes a sweet at random from the bag. If the sweet is red, the child stops taking sweets. If the sweet is green, it is not replaced and the child takes another sweet. This continues until a red sweet is taken, at which point the child stops taking sweets.
What is the probability that the child takes more green sweets than red sweets?
technique: Only one red sweet is ever taken, so "more green than red" means at least 2 green sweets are drawn before the red. $P(\text{red first})=\frac12$. $P(\text{green then red})=\frac{6}{12}\times\frac{6}{11}=\frac{3}{11}$. $P(\ge2\text{ green before red})=1-\frac12-\frac{3}{11}=\frac{22-11-6}{22}=\frac{5}{22}$.

steps.title: Only one red sweet is ever taken so
steps.content: Only one red sweet is ever taken, so "more green than red" means at least 2 green sweets are drawn before the red.

question:
Three detectors X, Y and Z are separated by large distances.
Each of the detectors records a seismic wave from the same earthquake whose epicentre (source) is very close to the surface of the Earth.
The wave travels out from the epicentre at 4.0 km s$^{-1}$.
Detectors X and Y start to detect the wave at the same time, but detector Z starts to detect it one minute later.
Which of the following statements must be correct?
1 The epicentre is at the midpoint of the line XY.
2 Z is equidistant from X and Y.
3 Z is no more than 240 km away from X and from Y.
technique: Statement 1 is false: X and Y detecting the wave simultaneously means the epicentre is equidistant from X and Y, i.e. it lies somewhere on the perpendicular bisector of XY — not necessarily at the midpoint of segment XY itself (that would require the epicentre to also lie exactly on line XY, which isn't given). Statement 2 is false: nothing constrains Z to be equidistant from X and Y — only that Z is 240km farther from the epicentre than X and Y are. Statement 3 is false: Z's straight-line distance from X or Y isn't bounded by the 240km extra path length in general, since the geometry is unconstrained. None of the three statements must be correct.

steps.title: Statement is false X and Y detecting the
steps.content: Statement 1 is false: X and Y detecting the wave simultaneously means the epicentre is equidistant from X and Y, i.e. it lies somewhere on the perpendicular bisector of XY — not necessarily at the midpoint of segment XY itself (that would require the epicentre to also lie exactly on line XY, which isn't given).

steps.title: Statement is false nothing constrains Z to be
steps.content: Statement 2 is false: nothing constrains Z to be equidistant from X and Y — only that Z is 240km farther from the epicentre than X and Y are.

steps.title: Statement is false Z s straight line distance
steps.content: Statement 3 is false: Z's straight-line distance from X or Y isn't bounded by the 240km extra path length in general, since the geometry is unconstrained.

steps.title: None of the three statements must be correct
steps.content: None of the three statements must be correct.

subtopic: Area between a parabola and a line
question:
Curve C has equation $y=9-x^2$.
Line L has equation $y=5$.
What is the area enclosed between C and L?
technique: Intersections: $9-x^2=5\Rightarrow x^2=4\Rightarrow x=\pm2$. Area $=\int_{-2}^{2}[(9-x^2)-5]\,dx=\int_{-2}^2(4-x^2)\,dx=\left[4x-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_{-2}^{2}=\left(8-\frac83\right)-\left(-8+\frac83\right)=16-\frac{16}{3}=\frac{32}{3}$.

question:
An aircraft moves from rest with uniform acceleration along a horizontal runway. After travelling 1600 m it reaches a speed of 80 m s$^{-1}$.
What is the acceleration of the aircraft?
technique: $v^2=u^2+2as\Rightarrow 80^2=0+2a(1600)\Rightarrow 6400=3200a\Rightarrow a=2.0$ m s$^{-2}$.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

subtopic: Trig equation with multiple solutions in an interval
question: How many solutions of the equation $2\sin^3\theta=\sin\theta$ lie in the interval $-\frac{\pi}{2}\le\theta\le\pi$?
technique: $\sin\theta(2\sin^2\theta-1)=0\Rightarrow\sin\theta=0$ or $\sin\theta=\pm\frac{1}{\sqrt2}$. In $[-\frac{\pi}{2},\pi]$: $\sin\theta=0$ gives $\theta=0,\pi$ (2 solutions); $\sin\theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt2}$ gives $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4},\frac{3\pi}{4}$ (2 solutions); $\sin\theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt2}$ gives $\theta=-\frac{\pi}{4}$ (1 solution, since $\frac{5\pi}{4}$ is out of range). Total $=2+2+1=5$.

steps.title: In gives solutions gives solutions gives solution since
steps.content: In $[-\frac{\pi}{2},\pi]$: $\sin\theta=0$ gives $\theta=0,\pi$ (2 solutions); $\sin\theta=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ gives $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4},\frac{3\pi}{4}$ (2 solutions); $\sin\theta=-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ gives $\theta=-\frac{\pi}{4}$ (1 solution, since $\frac{5\pi}{4}$ is out of range).

subtopic: Equilibrium of forces on an incline at constant velocity
question:
The diagram represents a mass that is moving in a straight line at constant speed up a slope of constant gradient.
[DIAGRAM: mass on an inclined slope moving up at constant speed]
Which statement about the forces acting on the mass must be correct?

options.A: All the forces acting on the mass are equal in magnitude.
options.B: Only three forces act on the mass.
options.C: The force of friction on the mass is equal to the driving force.
options.D: The weight of the mass acts in the opposite direction to the contact force.
options.E: There is no air resistance acting on the mass.
options.F: There is no resultant force acting on the mass.

technique: Constant velocity means zero acceleration, so by Newton's first/second law the resultant force on the mass must be zero, regardless of how many individual forces act.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Constant velocity means zero acceleration so by Newton
steps.content: Constant velocity means zero acceleration, so by Newton's first/second law the resultant force on the mass must be zero, regardless of how many individual forces act.

subtopic: Tangent line to a quadratic curve
question:
The line $y=x+k$, where $k$ is a constant, is a tangent to the curve $y=3x^2-2x+1$.
What is the value of $k$?
technique: Set equal: $x+k=3x^2-2x+1\Rightarrow3x^2-3x+(1-k)=0$. Tangency requires discriminant $=0$: $9-12(1-k)=0\Rightarrow9-12+12k=0\Rightarrow k=\frac{3}{12}=\frac14$.

subtopic: Connected particles, tension in a rod system
question:
The diagram shows four objects W, X, Y and Z, of masses 3.0 kg, 4.0kg, 6.0 kg and 2.0 kg respectively, connected by light, inextensible rods, in the order W-X-Y-Z.
The objects are pulled along a smooth, horizontal surface by a constant force of 30 N applied to W in the direction W to Z.
[DIAGRAM: W(3.0kg) — 30N force — X(4.0kg) — Y(6.0kg) — Z(2.0kg), connected in a line by rods]
What is the tension in the rod connecting X and Y?
technique: Total mass $=3+4+6+2=15$kg. Acceleration $a=30/15=2.0$ m s$^{-2}$. The rod between X and Y must accelerate the combined mass of Y and Z (8 kg): $T=8\times2.0=16$N.

steps.title: The rod between X and Y must accelerate
steps.content: The rod between X and Y must accelerate the combined mass of Y and Z (8 kg): $T=8\times2.0=16$N.

subtopic: Sector area and arc length with radians
question:
A sector S of a circle has area $10\pi$ cm$^2$.
The angle of sector S is increased by $\frac{\pi}{20}$ radians to form sector T.
The total area of sector T is $\frac{25\pi}{2}$ cm$^2$.
What is the total arc length, in cm, of sector T?
technique: Let radius $r$, angle $\theta$: $\frac12r^2\theta=10\pi$. Increasing angle by $\frac{\pi}{20}$ increases area by $\frac12r^2\cdot\frac{\pi}{20}=\frac{25\pi}{2}-10\pi=\frac{5\pi}{2}$, so $r^2=\frac{5\pi}{2}\times\frac{40}{\pi}=100\Rightarrow r=10$. Then $\theta=\dfrac{20\pi}{100}=\frac{\pi}{5}$, and sector T's angle $=\frac{\pi}{5}+\frac{\pi}{20}=\frac{\pi}{4}$. Arc length $=r\theta_T=10\times\frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{5\pi}{2}$.

steps.title: Increasing angle by increases area by so
steps.content: Increasing angle by $\frac{\pi}{20}$ increases area by $\frac12r^2\cdot\frac{\pi}{20}=\frac{25\pi}{2}-10\pi=\frac{5\pi}{2}$, so $r^2=\frac{5\pi}{2}\times\frac{40}{\pi}=100\Rightarrow r=10$.

steps.title: Then and sector T s angle

subtopic: Moments on a beam supported at two points
question:
An object of mass 40 kg is placed on a uniform, horizontal plank of mass 10 kg between two supports X and Y.
[DIAGRAM: plank of length 4.0m resting on supports X and Y, with the object placed 1.0m from X, the plank's centre 2.0m from X, and Y at the far end 1.0m beyond the centre]
What is the contact force at X?
(gravitational field strength = 10N kg$^{-1}$)
technique: Taking moments about Y, with weights $W_{object}=400$N at 1.0m from X (3.0m from Y), plank weight $=100$N at its centre (2.0m from Y), and support X at 4.0m from Y: balancing moments about Y and using force balance gives the contact force at X as 350N.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: Taking moments about Y with weights N at
steps.content: Taking moments about Y, with weights $W_{object}=400$N at 1.0m from X (3.0m from Y), plank weight $=100$N at its centre (2.0m from Y), and support X at 4.0m from Y: balancing moments about Y and using force balance gives the contact force at X as 350N.

subtopic: Arithmetic progression with two term conditions
question:
In a particular arithmetic progression:
• the 13th term is six times the 1st term
• the 11th term is 1 less than twice the 5th term
What is the 3rd term of the progression?
technique: Let first term $a$, common difference $d$. $a+12d=6a\Rightarrow12d=5a$. $a+10d=2(a+4d)-1\Rightarrow a+10d=2a+8d-1\Rightarrow2d-a=-1\Rightarrow a=2d+1$. Substituting: $12d=5(2d+1)=10d+5\Rightarrow2d=5\Rightarrow d=2.5$, so $a=6$. Third term $=a+2d=6+5=11$.

steps.title: Let first term common difference
steps.content: Let first term $a$, common difference $d$. $a+12d=6a\Rightarrow12d=5a$. $a+10d=2(a+4d)-1\Rightarrow a+10d=2a+8d-1\Rightarrow2d-a=-1\Rightarrow a=2d+1$.

subtopic: Energy conservation with friction on a slope
question:
A block of mass $m$ slides a distance $l$ down a slope that is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal.
The block experiences a friction force of $kW\sin\theta$, where $W$ is the weight of the block and $k$ is a constant.
The block starts from rest at the top of the slope and slides down a distance $l$ to the bottom, where its potential energy is zero.
What fraction of the initial potential energy at the top has become kinetic energy as the block reaches the bottom?
technique: Height drop $=l\sin\theta$, so PE lost $=mgl\sin\theta$. Work done against friction $=kW\sin\theta\times l=kmgl\sin\theta$. KE gained $=mgl\sin\theta-kmgl\sin\theta=mgl\sin\theta(1-k)$. Fraction of initial PE converted to KE $=1-k$.

steps.title: Fraction of initial PE converted to KE
steps.content: Fraction of initial PE converted to KE $=1-k$.

question:
The first three terms of a geometric progression, whose terms are all greater than zero, are $(p-2)$, $(2p+2)$ and $(5p+14)$.
What is the fifth term of the progression?
technique: Common ratio condition: $(2p+2)^2=(p-2)(5p+14)$. Expanding: $4p^2+8p+4=5p^2+4p-28\Rightarrow p^2-4p-32=0\Rightarrow(p-8)(p+4)=0$. $p=8$ gives terms $6,18,54$ (all positive, ratio 3); $p=-4$ gives all terms $-6$ (not positive, rejected). Fifth term $=6\times3^4=486$.

steps.title: Expanding gives terms all positive ratio gives all
steps.content: Expanding: $4p^2+8p+4=5p^2+4p-28\Rightarrow p^2-4p-32=0\Rightarrow(p-8)(p+4)=0$. $p=8$ gives terms $6,18,54$ (all positive, ratio 3); $p=-4$ gives all terms $-6$ (not positive, rejected).

question:
An object X of mass 2.0kg is initially moving at a speed of 4.5 m s$^{-1}$ on a smooth, horizontal surface.
A 5.0N force is applied to X in the direction of its motion for 3.0 seconds.
A short time later it collides head on with, and sticks to, a stationary object Y of mass 3.0 kg.
What is the speed of X and Y as they move off together after the collision?
technique: Acceleration of X: $a=F/m=5.0/2.0=2.5$ m s$^{-2}$. Speed before collision: $v=4.5+2.5\times3.0=12.0$ m s$^{-1}$. Momentum conservation in collision: $2.0\times12.0=(2.0+3.0)v_f\Rightarrow v_f=24/5=4.8$ m s$^{-1}$.

steps.title: Momentum conservation in collision m s
steps.content: Momentum conservation in collision: $2.0\times12.0=(2.0+3.0)v_f\Rightarrow v_f=24/5=4.8$ m s$^{-1}$.

question: Evaluate $\log_2\left(\dfrac{5}{4}\right)+\log_2\left(\dfrac{6}{5}\right)+\log_2\left(\dfrac{7}{6}\right)+\dots+\log_2\left(\dfrac{64}{63}\right)$
technique: The sum telescopes: $\log_2\left(\frac54\times\frac65\times\frac76\times\cdots\times\frac{64}{63}\right)=\log_2\left(\frac{64}{4}\right)=\log_2(16)=4$.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

subtopic: Kinematics and energy loss in a bounce
question:
A ball of mass 0.20kg is thrown vertically downwards at an initial speed of 4.0ms$^{-1}$ and travels a distance of 0.45 m to the ground.
The ball hits the ground, and rebounds with an initial speed of 2.0 ms$^{-1}$.
How much energy does the ball lose in the bounce?
(gravitational field strength = 10Nkg$^{-1}$; air resistance can be ignored)
technique: Speed just before impact: $v^2=4.0^2+2(10)(0.45)=16+9=25\Rightarrow v=5.0$ m s$^{-1}$. KE before bounce $=\frac12(0.20)(25)=2.5$J. KE after bounce $=\frac12(0.20)(2.0)^2=0.4$J. Energy lost $=2.5-0.4=2.1$J.

steps.title: Speed just before impact m s
steps.content: Speed just before impact: $v^2=4.0^2+2(10)(0.45)=16+9=25\Rightarrow v=5.0$ m s$^{-1}$.

question:
Circle C has equation $(x+3)^2+(y-2)^2=5$.
The length of the tangent from the circle C to the point P is $5\sqrt3$.
What is the shortest distance from P to C?
technique: Radius $r=\sqrt5$. For a tangent from external point P: (tangent length)$^2=d^2-r^2$, where $d$ is the distance from P to the centre. $(5\sqrt3)^2=75=d^2-5\Rightarrow d^2=80\Rightarrow d=4\sqrt5$. Shortest distance from P to the circle $=d-r=4\sqrt5-\sqrt5=3\sqrt5$.

steps.title: For a tangent from external point P tangent
steps.content: For a tangent from external point P: (tangent length)$^2=d^2-r^2$, where $d$ is the distance from P to the centre. $(5\sqrt{3})^2=75=d^2-5\Rightarrow d^2=80\Rightarrow d=4\sqrt{5}$.

steps.title: Shortest distance from P to the circle
steps.content: Shortest distance from P to the circle $=d-r=4\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{5}=3\sqrt{5}$.

subtopic: Momentum conservation in a collision (velocity-time graph)
question:
Two solid spheres X and Y have masses $m$ and $2m$ respectively. They travel in opposite directions towards each other along the same line with speeds $v$ and $2v$ respectively and collide head on.
The graph shows the variation of velocity with time for sphere X before, during, and after the collision: X starts at velocity $+v$ and ends at velocity $-v$.
[DIAGRAM: velocity-time graph for sphere X, showing a step from +v before collision to -v after collision]
Which sketch shows the variation of velocity with time for sphere Y?

options.A: graph showing Y going from $2v$ to $-2v$
options.B: graph showing Y going from $-2v$ to $2v$
options.C: graph showing Y going from $2v$ to $-v$
options.D: graph showing Y going from $-2v$ to $v$
options.E: graph showing Y going from $2v$ to $v$
options.F: graph showing Y going from $-2v$ to $-v$

technique: Taking X's initial direction as positive, X: $+v\to-v$, Y (opposite direction): $-2v$ initially. Momentum conservation: $m(v)+2m(-2v)=m(-v)+2m(v_Y')\Rightarrow -3mv=-mv+2mv_Y'\Rightarrow2mv_Y'=-2mv\Rightarrow v_Y'=-v$. So Y goes from $-2v$ to $-v$.

steps.title: Taking X s initial direction as positive X
steps.content: Taking X's initial direction as positive, X: $+v\to-v$, Y (opposite direction): $-2v$ initially.

subtopic: Distance formula with a ratio condition
question:
The points $A(-3, 2)$, $B(1, 3)$ and $C(-1, u)$ are such that the distances AC and AB are related by:
$AC=2AB$
What are the possible values of $u$?
technique: $AB=\sqrt{(1-(-3))^2+(3-2)^2}=\sqrt{17}$. $AC=2AB\Rightarrow AC^2=4\times17=68$. $AC^2=(-1-(-3))^2+(u-2)^2=4+(u-2)^2=68\Rightarrow(u-2)^2=64\Rightarrow u-2=\pm8\Rightarrow u=10$ or $u=-6$.

subtopic: Equilibrium of a suspended mass with horizontal force
question:
A metal ball suspended from a steel cable is held at rest by a horizontal force P. The cable makes an angle of 30° to the vertical. The cable exerts a force T on the ball.
[DIAGRAM: ball hanging from a cable at 30° to the vertical, held by horizontal force P]
What is the magnitude of P?
technique: Resolving horizontally at equilibrium: $P=T\sin30°=\dfrac{T}{2}$.

steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

question: What is the coefficient of $x^3$ in the expansion of $(1-2x)^5(1+2x)^5$?
technique: $(1-2x)^5(1+2x)^5=[(1-2x)(1+2x)]^5=(1-4x^2)^5$, which only contains even powers of $x$. So the coefficient of $x^3$ is $0$.

steps.title: Which only contains even powers of
steps.content: $(1-2x)^5(1+2x)^5=[(1-2x)(1+2x)]^5=(1-4x^2)^5$, which only contains even powers of $x$.

question:
A pendulum bob of mass 10 g is suspended by a light, inextensible string of length 50cm.
The bob is released from rest at position X.
[DIAGRAM: pendulum of length 50cm, bob released from X and swinging to Y, with the bob 30cm below the pivot's horizontal level at Y]
What is the speed of the bob as it passes through position Y?
(gravitational field strength g = 10Nkg$^{-1}$; assume that resistive forces are negligible)
technique: Vertical drop in height from X to Y $=50-30=20$cm$=0.2$m. Energy conservation: $\frac12mv^2=mgh\Rightarrow v=\sqrt{2gh}=\sqrt{2\times10\times0.2}=\sqrt4=2$ m s$^{-1}$.

steps.title: Vertical drop in height from X to Y
steps.content: Vertical drop in height from X to Y $=50-30=20$cm$=0.2$m.

subtopic: Definite integrals, solving for unknown limit
question: Given that $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m}\,dx = \frac{16\sqrt{2}}{7}$ and $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m+1}\,dx = \frac{32\sqrt{2}}{9}$, what is the value of $m$?
technique: Integrate directly: $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m}\,dx = \left[\frac{x^{m+1}}{m+1}\right]_0^{2} = \frac{2^{m+1}}{m+1}$, and likewise $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m+1}\,dx = \frac{2^{m+2}}{m+2}$. Rather than solving either equation alone, divide the second by the first — the awkward $\sqrt{2}$ and the powers of $2$ both cancel: $\dfrac{2^{m+2}}{m+2}\cdot\dfrac{m+1}{2^{m+1}} = \dfrac{2(m+1)}{m+2}$, while the right-hand sides give $\dfrac{32\sqrt{2}}{9}\cdot\dfrac{7}{16\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{14}{9}$. So $\dfrac{2(m+1)}{m+2} = \dfrac{14}{9}$, hence $18(m+1) = 14(m+2)$, giving $4m = 10$ and $m = \dfrac{5}{2}$. Check: $m+1 = \tfrac{7}{2}$ and $2^{7/2} = 8\sqrt{2}$, so the first integral is $\dfrac{8\sqrt{2}}{7/2} = \dfrac{16\sqrt{2}}{7}$ as required.

steps.title: Integrate directly and likewise x m dx
steps.content: Integrate directly: $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m}\,dx = \left[\frac{x^{m+1}}{m+1}\right]_0^{2} = \frac{2^{m+1}}{m+1}$, and likewise $\displaystyle\int_0^{2} x^{m+1}\,dx

steps.title: Rather than solving either equation alone divide the
steps.content: Rather than solving either equation alone, divide the second by the first — the awkward $\sqrt{2}$ and the powers of $2$ both cancel: $\dfrac{2^{m+2}}{m+2}\cdot\dfrac{m+1}{2^{m+1}} = \dfrac{2(m+1)}{m+2}$, while the right-hand sides give $\dfrac{32\sqrt{2}}{9}\cdot\dfrac{7}{16\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{14}{9}$.

steps.content: Check: $m+1 = \tfrac{7}{2}$ and $2^{7/2} = 8\sqrt{2}$, so the first integral is $\dfrac{8\sqrt{2}}{7/2}

question:
An object of mass $m$ is initially moving at constant speed $u$ to the right. It collides with a stationary object of greater mass $M$ and bounces back in the opposite direction at speed $v$.
What is the speed of the greater mass immediately after the collision?
technique: Momentum conservation: $mu=-mv+MV\Rightarrow MV=m(u+v)\Rightarrow V=\dfrac{m(u+v)}{M}$.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

question:
The two functions $f$ and $g$ satisfy
$f'(x)=ax+g(x)$
where $a$ is a constant.
Given that $\displaystyle\int_2^4 g(x)\,dx=12$ and $f(4)=18+f(2)$, what is the value of $a$?
technique: $f(4)-f(2)=\displaystyle\int_2^4 f'(x)\,dx=\int_2^4(ax+g(x))\,dx=a\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_2^4+12=a(8-2)+12=6a+12$. Given $f(4)-f(2)=18$: $6a+12=18\Rightarrow a=1$.

question:
A load drops from rest through a vertical height $h$ to the ground.
A light cable attached to the load passes over a friction pulley that provides a braking force during the fall.
As the load falls through height $h$, 50% of the gravitational potential energy lost is transferred into thermal energy.
The load reaches a final speed of 10 ms$^{-1}$.
[DIAGRAM: load falling height h, cable over a friction pulley]
What is the vertical height $h$?
(gravitational field strength g = 10Nkg$^{-1}$; air resistance can be ignored)
technique: PE lost $=mgh$; half becomes thermal, half becomes KE: $\frac12mgh=\frac12mv^2\Rightarrow gh=v^2\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{v^2}{g}=\dfrac{100}{10}=10$m.

steps.title: Begin from the given information and apply the
steps.content: Begin from the given information and apply the relevant relation.

steps.title: PE lost half becomes thermal half becomes KE
steps.content: PE lost $=mgh$; half becomes thermal, half becomes KE: $\frac12mgh=\frac12mv^2\Rightarrow gh=v^2\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{v^2}{g}=\dfrac{100}{10}=10$m.

subtopic: Optimisation: maximising surface area for fixed volume
question:
The dimensions of a solid cuboid, in cm, are $x$, $2x$ and $y$.
The volume of the cuboid is 576cm$^3$.
At this volume, the surface area of the cuboid has its maximum value (i.e. the surface area is stationary with respect to $x$ for the fixed volume).
What is the area, in cm$^2$, of the face that has the largest area?
technique: $V=2x^2y=576\Rightarrow y=288/x^2$. $SA=2(x\cdot2x+2x\cdot y+x\cdot y)=4x^2+6xy=4x^2+\dfrac{1728}{x}$. Setting $\dfrac{d(SA)}{dx}=8x-\dfrac{1728}{x^2}=0\Rightarrow x^3=216\Rightarrow x=6$. Then $y=288/36=8$. Faces: $x\times2x=72$, $x\times y=48$, $2x\times y=96$. Largest face area $=96$cm$^2$.

question:
An object is thrown vertically upwards from ground level with an initial velocity of 40 ms$^{-1}$.
2.0 seconds later another object is released from a height above the ground and falls vertically from rest.
Both of the objects hit the ground at the same time.
From what height above the ground was the second object released?
(gravitational field strength g = 10Nkg$^{-1}$; air resistance can be ignored)
technique: First object: $s=40t-5t^2=0\Rightarrow t=8$s (time to return to ground, excluding $t=0$). Second object is released at $t=2$s and falls for $8-2=6$s. Height $h=\frac12g t^2=\frac12(10)(6)^2=180$m.

steps.title: First object s time to return to ground
steps.content: First object: $s=40t-5t^2=0\Rightarrow t=8$s (time to return to ground, excluding $t=0$).

steps.title: Second object is released at s and falls
steps.content: Second object is released at $t=2$s and falls for $8-2=6$s.
